Alright, I'm trying to figure out whether the mutant code change in DateTimeZone.java at line 77 affects adjacent fault conditions. The mutant changed lines 77-83 as follows:

Original:
if (id.startsWith("+") || id.startsWith("-")) {
    int offset = parseOffset(id);
    if (offset == 0L) {
        return DateTimeZone.UTC;
    } else {
        id = printOffset(offset);
    }
}

Mutant changed to:
if (id.startsWith("+") || id.startsWith("-")) {
    int offset = parseOffset(id);
    if (false) {
        return DateTimeZone.UTC;
    } else {
        id = printOffset(offset);
    }
}

So, the change is that the condition inside the if statement was "offset == 0L", which now is replaced by a constant false. That means regardless of the offset value, it will always go into the else block and set id to printOffset(offset). 

Looking at the failing tests after this mutation:

- TestMonthDay_Properties#testPropertyGetMonthOfYear: status changed from failing to passing (fixed).
- All other DateTimeZoneCutover tests remained failing.

This indicates that the mutant affected these tests. The test that was fixed is likely related to month name localization, possibly because the id processing no longer affects it. However, the other tests are still failing, suggesting issues with timezone offset handling.

The question is whether this mutation could be adjacent to a fault, meaning if fixing one issue might introduce another nearby. Since the mutant changed the condition from checking offset to always false, it's possible that other parts of the code relying on the UTC return when offset is zero are now broken. 

In particular, tests like testBug2182444_ausNSW and others expect specific offsets but might not be getting them correctly anymore because the id isn't being set to UTC when appropriate. This could mean that the mutation introduced a new bug adjacent to where it was applied.

So, I think this mutant is fault-adjacent because changing the condition altered the flow in a way that affects multiple tests, possibly introducing new issues or uncovering existing ones nearby.
</think>
